docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Adobe Photoshop is a professional image editing application used by designers, photographers, and artists to edit photos, design graphics, and create digital artwork. The purpose of this document is to help beginners with Photoshop by providing step-by-step instructions for four key tasks: applying effects, non-destructive editing, image retouching, and working with vectors. This guide is intended for users with little to no prior Photoshop experience who want to build foundational skills. No advanced knowledge is required — just follow each step in order and refer to the Glossary or Troubleshooting sections if needed.

docs.title: Is This Guide for You?
docs.text: This guide is created for any beginner student at BCIT who uses a Windows device and has little to no experience with Photoshop. We will go through detailed, step-by-step instructions to help you get familiar with the most common functions you will need in Photoshop.

docs.title: By the End of This Guide
docs.text:
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

  1. Create, apply, and customize Layer Styles
  2. Work non-destructively instead of destructively
  3. Retouch a photo using key tools
  4. Create and edit vector shapes using the Pen Tool

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
To follow these instructions, you will need:

  • Windows 11 operating system
  • Adobe Photoshop v27.x (recommended)
  • A BCIT email account to download and log in to Photoshop
  • Basic file management skills: creating, opening, and saving files in Photoshop

docs.title: Typographical Conventions
docs.text:
The following conventions are used throughout this guide:

Convention Meaning Example Bold text Buttons, menu items, or actions to perform Click the Layer menu Monospace text Keyboard shortcuts Ctrl + Z Italic text New or important Photoshop terms layer mask [Menu] → [Option] A sequence of menu clicks [Filter] → [Blur] → [Gaussian Blur]

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text:
The following admonitions are used throughout this guide:

Note

Provides helpful tips or extra context to support your understanding.

Warning

Indicates an action that could cause data loss or an irreversible change.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
This glossary defines key terms used throughout the Non-Destructive Editing and Vector Shapes sections of this guide. Terms are listed in alphabetical order.

Term Definition Adjustment Layer A special layer that applies color or tone changes (such as Hue/Saturation) to all layers below it without permanently altering any pixels. Adjustment Layers can be modified, hidden, or deleted at any time. Anchor Point A small dot placed on the canvas when using the Pen Tool or Shape Tool. Anchor points define the corners and curves of a vector path and can be moved at any time using the Direct Selection Tool. Background Layer The default bottom layer created when you open or create a new file in Photoshop. The Background layer is locked by default and should never be edited directly — always duplicate it first to protect the original. Close Path The action of connecting the last anchor point of a Pen Tool path back to the first anchor point, creating a fully enclosed shape. A closed path is required before a vector shape can be filled with a solid color. Curve Handle A directional control that appears when you click and drag with the Pen Tool to place a curved anchor point. Dragging the curve handle changes the direction and intensity of the curve between two anchor points. Destructive Editing Any edit that permanently changes the pixels of an image, such as applying Hue/Saturation directly via [Image] → [Adjustments]. Destructive edits cannot be undone once the file is saved and closed. Direct Selection Tool A Photoshop tool (shortcut: A) used to select and move individual anchor points on a vector shape. Unlike the regular Selection Tool which moves the entire layer, this tool lets you adjust just one part of a vector path. Duplicate Layer A copy of an existing layer created by pressing Ctrl + J or right-clicking the layer and selecting [Duplicate Layer]. Working on a duplicate protects the original layer from accidental changes. Fill The solid color, gradient, or pattern applied to the inside of a vector shape. The fill color is set in the top options bar before or after drawing a shape using the Shape Tool. Gaussian Blur A filter found under [Filter] → [Blur] → [Gaussian Blur] that softens an image by blending pixel values together. When applied to a Smart Object, it becomes a Smart Filter that can be adjusted or removed at any time. Hue/Saturation A color adjustment that controls the hue (color tone), saturation (color intensity), and lightness of an image. It can be applied destructively via [Image] → [Adjustments] or non-destructively via an Adjustment Layer. Layers Panel The panel in Photoshop (usually on the right side of the screen) that displays all layers in the current document. Each layer can be hidden, reordered, renamed, or deleted from this panel. Non-Destructive Editing An approach to editing that preserves the original image pixels at all times using Adjustment Layers, Smart Objects, and Smart Filters. All non-destructive changes can be modified or removed without permanently affecting the original image. Path A line drawn between two anchor points using the Pen Tool. Paths define the outline of a vector shape and can be straight or curved depending on how the anchor points were placed. Pen Tool A Photoshop tool (shortcut: P) used to draw custom vector shapes by manually placing anchor points on the canvas. Clicking creates sharp corner points, while clicking and dragging creates smooth curved points. PSD The native file format for Adobe Photoshop (.psd). Saving as PSD preserves all layers, Smart Objects, Adjustment Layers, and vector data so they remain fully editable. Raster An image made up of individual pixels arranged in a grid. Raster images lose quality and become blurry when scaled up because there are a fixed number of pixels — most photographs are raster images. Shape Layer A vector-based layer created when you draw a shape using the Shape Tool or Pen Tool. Shape Layers store the shape as a mathematical path rather than pixels, meaning it can be resized to any size without quality loss. Shape Tool A Photoshop tool (shortcut: U) that lets you draw pre-built vector shapes such as rectangles, ellipses, triangles, stars, and polygons. The top options bar must be set to Shape mode for the result to be saved as a vector layer. Smart Filter A filter applied to a Smart Object layer that remains fully editable after it is applied. Smart Filters appear underneath the Smart Object in the Layers panel and can be double-clicked to adjust or removed entirely at any time. Smart Object A special layer type that wraps the original content inside a protected container, preventing permanent changes. Filters applied to a Smart Object become Smart Filters, and the original image data is never altered. Stroke An outline or border applied to the outside edge of a vector shape. The stroke color and thickness are set in the top options bar alongside the fill color when using the Shape Tool. Vector A type of graphic defined by mathematical paths and anchor points rather than pixels. Vectors can be scaled to any size without losing sharpness or quality, making them ideal for logos, icons, and illustrations.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: This guide covers the most common issues you may run into while following the Non-Destructive Editing and Vector Shapes tutorials, along with detailed steps to fix each one.

docs.title: 1. The Layers Panel Is Not Visible On Screen
docs.text:
If you cannot see the Layers panel, you will not be able to duplicate your Background layer, add Adjustment Layers, or manage Smart Objects, all of which are required for these tutorials.

Fix:

  1. Go to [Window] in the top menu bar
  2. Click [Layers] - the Layers panel will appear on your screen
  3. If it opens as a floating panel, drag it toward the right edge of your screen until it attaches and stops floating

Note

You will know it worked when you can see a panel on the right side of your screen showing a row labelled Background with a small padlock icon next to it.

docs.title: 2. The Shape Tool Is Drawing a Path Outline or Flat Pixels Instead of a Scalable Vector Shape
docs.text:
After drawing your shape, it appears either as a thin outline with no fill, or as a flat coloured area you can no longer resize or edit. This happens because the mode is set to Path or Pixels instead of Shape.

Fix:

  1. Look at the horizontal bar that runs along the very top of your screen, just below the menu bar - this is the options bar
  2. On the far left of that bar, you will see a dropdown that reads either Shape, Path, or Pixels
  3. Click it and select Shape
  4. If you have already drawn your shape incorrectly, press Ctrl + Z to undo, change the mode to Shape, and redraw it

Note

You will know it worked when small blue anchor points appear at the corners and edges of your shape after you release the mouse.

docs.title: 3. The Pen Tool Creates a New Anchor Point Instead of Closing the Shape
docs.text:
When you try to close your path, clicking near the first anchor point places a new point instead, leaving your shape open and unfilled.

Fix:

  1. Hover your cursor very slowly and precisely over the exact first anchor point you placed
  2. Watch closely for a small circle icon to appear next to the pen cursor, this is the only visual signal that Photoshop is ready to close the path
  3. Do not click until you see that circle
  4. If you clicked too early and created an extra point by mistake, press Ctrl + Z immediately to undo, then hover over the first point again more carefully before clicking

Note

You will know it worked when your shape becomes a fully enclosed, filled area with no open gaps or loose lines.

docs.title: 4. Gaussian Blur Appears as a Permanent Change Instead of an Editable Smart Filter
docs.text:
After applying [Filter] → [Blur] → [Gaussian Blur], the blur is applied directly to the layer with no Smart Filter appearing underneath it in the Layers panel. This means the layer was not converted to a Smart Object before the filter was applied.

Fix:

  1. Press Ctrl + Z to undo the blur
  2. In the Layers panel, right-click your layer and select [Convert to Smart Object] - you will know the conversion worked when a small square icon appears on the small image preview square on the left side of your layer row
  3. Reapply the filter via [Filter] → [Blur] → [Gaussian Blur] and click [OK]

Note

You will know it worked when the words Gaussian Blur appear as a separate, clickable line directly below your layer in the Layers panel. You can double-click that line at any time to adjust the blur radius.

docs.title: 5. All Layers and Edits Are Lost After Saving
docs.text:
After reopening your file, everything appears flattened into a single image - all Adjustment Layers, Smart Objects, and Smart Filters are gone and can no longer be edited.

Fix:

  1. Always save your working file via [File] → [Save As]
  2. Set the format to Photoshop (.psd)
  3. Click [Save]

Note

You will know it worked when you reopen the file and can still see all your individual layers listed separately in the Layers panel, rather than a single flattened image.

Warning

Saving as .jpg or .png permanently flattens all layers into pixels and cannot be undone. Only export to .jpg or .png when you have a fully finished version that you no longer need to edit - and even then, always keep your .psd file as your master copy.

docs.title: 6. A Warning Triangle Icon Appears Next to My Selected Color in the Color Picker
docs.text:
While selecting a fill color for your shape in the Color Picker, a small triangle with an exclamation mark (⚠️) appears next to your chosen color. This can look alarming, but it is not an error, it is Photoshop's out-of-gamut warning, which means the color you have selected may not print accurately on a physical printer.

Fix:

  1. If you are working on a digital project (anything viewed on a screen), you can safely ignore this warning entirely, it has no effect on how your color looks on screen
  2. Simply click [OK] to confirm your color selection and continue

Note

You will know everything is fine when the Color Picker closes and your chosen color appears correctly in the Fill box in the top options bar, ready for you to draw your shape.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:

1.

docs.title: Before You Begin
docs.text:
Note

Warning

docs.title: Step 1: Pick an image you like to fix
Overview
docs.text: Non-destructive editing allows you to make changes to an image without permanently altering the original pixels. Instead of editing directly on the image, you work with Adjustment Layers and Smart Objects — meaning you can always undo, tweak, or remove any change at any point without losing quality. This is the recommended way to edit in Photoshop, especially for beginners, as it gives you full creative freedom without the risk of ruining your original image.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:

  1. Understand the difference between destructive and non-destructive editing
  2. Use Adjustment Layers to edit colors and tones safely
  3. Convert a layer into a Smart Object to protect the original image

docs.title: Before You Begin
docs.text:
Warning

Avoid editing directly on the Background layer — always duplicate it first by pressing Ctrl + J.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This section will walk you through each step of non-destructive editing in Photoshop, from opening your image to saving your final file.

docs.title: Step 1: Download the Practice Image
docs.text:
Download the practice image here: Click to Download

Figure 1: Practice image — British Shorthair cat with bow tie

Once downloaded, locate the file in your Downloads folder — it will be saved as a .jpg file.

docs.title: Step 2: Open the Image in Photoshop
docs.text:
1. Open Photoshop
  2. Click [File] → [Open]
  3. Navigate to where you downloaded the image
  4. Select the image and click [Open]

Note

You can also drag and drop the image directly into Photoshop.

docs.title: Step 3: Duplicate the Background Layer
docs.text:
Before making any edits, always duplicate your original layer to protect it. If you do not see the Layers panel, go to [Window] → [Layers] to open it.

  1. Right-click on the Background layer in the Layers panel

  2. Select [Duplicate Layer] or press Ctrl + J

    Figure 2: Duplicating the Background layer

Warning

Never edit directly on the Background layer — always work on a duplicate.

docs.title: Step 4: The Destructive Way (What NOT to Do)
docs.text:
This section demonstrates what happens when you edit destructively, so you understand why it should be avoided.

  1. Click [Image] → [Adjustments] → [Hue/Saturation]
  2. Drag the Hue slider to any value

  3. Click [OK]

    Figure 3: Adjusting Hue/Saturation destructively

    Figure 4: The image is permanently changed

Warning

This permanently changes your image pixels. If you close and reopen the file, you cannot undo this change.

Warning

Before continuing, press Ctrl + Z to undo this change, or close the file without saving and reopen it.

docs.title: Step 5: The Non-Destructive Way (The Right Way)
docs.text:
Instead of editing the image directly, we will add an Adjustment Layer on top.

  1. Go to [Layer] → [New Adjustment Layer] → [Hue/Saturation]

  2. Click [OK] on the dialog that appears

    Figure 5: Adding a Hue/Saturation Adjustment Layer

  3. Drag the Hue slider to -137

    Figure 6: Adjusting the Hue slider

  4. Click the eye icon (the small eye symbol on the far left of the layer row) next to the Adjustment Layer in the Layers panel to toggle it on and off

    Figure 7: Toggling the Adjustment Layer on and off

  5. Double-click the Adjustment Layer thumbnail at any time to go back and change the values

    Figure 8: Re-editing the Adjustment Layer

Note

The Adjustment Layer sits above your image and never touches the original pixels — you can modify or delete it at any time.

docs.title: Step 6: Rename Your Layers
docs.text:
Keeping your layers organized will save you a lot of confusion later.

  1. Double-click the duplicated layer's name in the Layers panel
  2. Type a descriptive name such as Editing Layer

  3. Press Enter to confirm

    Figure 9: Renaming the layer

docs.title: Step 7: Convert the Layer to a Smart Object
docs.text:
Converting to a Smart Object allows you to apply filters non-destructively.

  1. Right-click on your duplicated layer in the Layers panel

  2. Click [Convert to Smart Object]

    Figure 10: Converting the layer to a Smart Object

  3. Confirm the conversion by looking for the small icon on the layer thumbnail

    Figure 11: Smart Object icon confirming the conversion

Note

The small icon on the layer thumbnail confirms it is now a Smart Object. This means any filters you apply will be editable and non-destructive. When you double-click on it, Photoshop opens the Smart Object as a separate file (.psb). You can move any layers or content related to your edit into this Smart Object to keep things organized. Any edits made inside will apply back to your original file. Save and close the tab when done to see your changes reflected.

docs.title: Step 8: Apply a Filter to the Smart Object
docs.text:
1. Click [Filter] → [Blur] → [Gaussian Blur]

    Figure 12: Opening the Gaussian Blur filter

  2. Drag the radius slider to around 5–10 pixels to see a visible blur effect on your image

  3. Click [OK]

    Figure 13: Adjusting the Gaussian Blur radius

Note

Because this is a Smart Object, the filter appears as a Smart Filter underneath the layer — it is fully editable at any time.

Figure 14: Smart Filter visible in the Layers panel

docs.title: Step 9: Edit the Smart Filter
docs.text:
After applying the Gaussian Blur in Step 8, you will see the filter name listed directly beneath the Smart Object layer in the Layers panel.

  1. Double-click the Smart Filter name (e.g., Gaussian Blur) in the Layers panel
  2. Adjust the slider to a new value
  3. Click [OK]

Note

This is the power of non-destructive editing — you can always go back and change any filter without starting over.

docs.title: Step 10: Save Your File
docs.text:
To preserve all your layers and edits, save as a Photoshop file.

  1. Click [File] → [Save As]
  2. Set the format to .psd
  3. Click [Save]

Warning

Saving as .jpg or .png will flatten all your layers and you will lose all your non-destructive edits permanently.

docs.title: Bonus
docs.text:
To test your understanding, here is a finished Photoshop image that you should be able to recreate after going through this tutorial. It involves everything you have been taught.

Figure 15: Completed example using Adjustment Layers and Smart Objects

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You have successfully completed the Non-Destructive Editing tutorial. You now know how to use Adjustment Layers and Smart Objects to edit images safely in Photoshop without ever permanently altering your original image. These techniques are the foundation of professional photo editing and will be used throughout the rest of your careers.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Retouching images in Adobe Photoshop is the skill of enhancing and correcting photos to improve their visual quality while maintaining a natural look. It is a common task people use adobe photoshop for. This skill requires attention to detail, an understanding of composition and color, and the ability to subtly refine images so the final result looks polished and professional without appearing overly edited.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:

1.

docs.title: Before You Begin
docs.text:
Note

Warning

docs.title: Step 1: Pick an image you like to fix
Overview
docs.text: Vectors are shapes defined by mathematical paths rather than pixels, meaning they can be scaled to any size without losing quality. Unlike regular photos or raster images, vector shapes will never become blurry or pixelated no matter how large or small you make them. In Photoshop, you can create vectors using the Shape Tool and Pen Tool — making them ideal for logos, icons, buttons, and any graphic element that needs to look sharp at any size.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:

  1. Understand the difference between vector and raster graphics
  2. Create a simple vector shape using the Shape Tool
  3. Draw a custom vector path using the Pen Tool
  4. Adjust and fine-tune your vector shape using the Direct Selection Tool

docs.title: Before You Begin
docs.text:
Note

This tutorial will guide you through creating a new canvas from scratch. No existing file is required — just open Photoshop and follow along from Step 1.

Note

Vector shapes in Photoshop are stored as Shape Layers — they appear in your Layers panel just like regular layers. This means you can always go back, select the layer, and edit the shape at any time without losing quality.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This section will walk you through creating and editing vector shapes in Photoshop from scratch. The tutorial is split into two parts — Part 1 covers creating a basic shape using the Shape Tool, and Part 2 covers drawing a fully custom shape using the Pen Tool. Follow each step carefully, as the Pen Tool in particular takes practice to get used to.

docs.title: Part 1: Creating a Shape Using the Shape Tool
docs.text: The Shape Tool is the easiest way to create vector shapes in Photoshop. It gives you a selection of pre-built shapes like rectangles, circles, stars, and polygons that you can place onto your canvas instantly without having to draw anything by hand.

docs.title: Step 1: Open Photoshop and Create a New File
docs.text:
When you open Photoshop, you will land on the Home screen. From here, you need to create a new blank canvas to work on — think of it as setting up a blank sheet of paper before you start drawing.

  1. Open Photoshop — you will see the Home screen with options to create or open a file

    Figure 1: Photoshop Home screen

  2. Click [File] → [New] or press Ctrl + N to open the new file dialog

    Figure 2: Navigating to File → New

  3. Set Width to 1920, Height to 1080, Resolution to 72, and Color Mode to RGB Color, then click [Create]

Note

You can use any canvas size for this tutorial — the default settings work fine for beginners. The canvas size just determines how large your working area is and does not affect the quality of your vector shapes.

docs.title: Step 2: Select the Shape Tool
docs.text:
The Shape Tool is hidden in the left toolbar alongside several other shape options. You need to right-click the toolbar icon to reveal the full list of available shapes, because each shape type creates a different kind of vector — a rectangle, a circle, a star, and so on.

  1. Right-click the Shape Tool icon in the left toolbar — it looks like a small rectangle

  2. Select [Rectangle Tool] from the dropdown menu — you will see options including Ellipse, Triangle, Polygon, Star, Line, and Custom Shape

    Figure 3: Shape Tool options in the toolbar

Note

You can press U on your keyboard to quickly select the Shape Tool. If you press Shift + U it will cycle through the different shape options, so you can switch between rectangle, ellipse, star, and more without touching the mouse.

docs.title: Step 3: Set the Fill Color
docs.text:
Before you draw your shape, it is important to choose a fill color first. The fill color is what appears inside your shape — without it, your shape will look transparent or completely invisible on the canvas.

  1. Click the Fill color box in the top options bar — it is located at the top left of your screen once the Shape Tool is selected

  2. The Color Picker dialog will open — by default it shows black (#000000)

    Figure 4: Color Picker dialog with default black color

  3. Type a hex color code in the # field at the bottom (e.g., 3760be for blue) or drag the color sliders to visually pick a color you like

  4. Click [OK] to confirm your selected color

    Figure 5: Selecting a blue fill color (#3760be)

Note

If you do not see the Fill option at the top, make sure the Rectangle Tool is still selected — the options bar changes depending on which tool is active.

Note

The new and current boxes at the top of the Color Picker let you compare your new color against the previous one before confirming. The small triangle (⚠️) that may appear is Photoshop's out-of-gamut warning — it means the color may not print accurately, but is safe to ignore for digital projects. Click [OK] to continue.

docs.title: Step 4: Draw the Shape
docs.text:
Now that your tool and color are set up, you are ready to draw your first vector shape. Drawing a shape in Photoshop is as simple as clicking and dragging across the canvas — Photoshop automatically creates it as a vector Shape Layer that you can resize and edit at any time.

  1. Click and drag on the canvas to draw your rectangle — the further you drag, the larger the shape will be

  2. Release the mouse when the shape is the size you want — you will see small blue anchor points appear at the corners and edges, confirming it has been created as a vector

    Figure 6: A rectangle vector shape drawn on the canvas

Note

Hold Shift while dragging to constrain the shape proportions — this forces a rectangle to become a perfect square, or an ellipse to become a perfect circle. Release Shift after you release the mouse to keep the proportions locked.

Warning

Make sure the mode in the top options bar is set to Shape and not Path or Pixels — otherwise your shape will not be saved as a vector layer and you will lose the ability to scale it without quality loss.

docs.title: Part 2: Drawing a Custom Path Using the Pen Tool
docs.text: The Pen Tool is one of the most powerful — and most challenging — tools in Photoshop. Unlike the Shape Tool which gives you pre-made shapes, the Pen Tool lets you draw any shape you can imagine by manually placing individual anchor points on the canvas. Each anchor point is connected to the next by a path, and together they form the outline of your fully custom vector shape.

docs.title: Step 5: Select the Pen Tool
docs.text:
The Pen Tool is located in the left toolbar and has several sub-tools hidden underneath it. For this tutorial, you will use the standard Pen Tool, which gives you the most control over your paths and anchor points compared to the other Pen Tool variations.

  1. Right-click the Pen Tool icon in the left toolbar — it looks like the tip of a fountain pen nib

  2. Select [Pen Tool] from the dropdown menu — you will see options for Freeform Pen, Curvature Pen, and Anchor Point tools

    Figure 7: Pen Tool options in the toolbar

Note

You can press P on your keyboard to quickly select the Pen Tool. The other tools in the dropdown (like Curvature Pen and Freeform Pen) are easier to use but give you less precise control — stick to the standard Pen Tool for now until you are comfortable with the basics.

docs.title: Step 6: Draw Anchor Points to Create a Path
docs.text:
The Pen Tool works very differently from a regular drawing tool. Instead of drawing freely, you place individual anchor points on the canvas one click at a time, and Photoshop draws a straight or curved path between each one. Understanding the difference between a regular click and a click-and-drag is the most important concept to grasp when using the Pen Tool.

Warning

Before clicking, make sure the mode in the top options bar is set to Shape and not Path — otherwise your pen path will not create a filled vector layer.

Figure 8: Top options bar set to Shape mode

  1. Click anywhere on the canvas to place your first anchor point — this is the starting point of your shape
  2. Click a second location on the canvas to place another anchor point — Photoshop will automatically draw a straight line connecting the two points

  3. Click and drag instead of just clicking to create a curved path between points — as you drag, you will see a curve handle appear that controls the direction and intensity of the curve

    Figure 9: A curved path created with two anchor points

Note

Clicking creates a sharp corner point, while click and dragging creates a smooth curved point. Most custom shapes use a combination of both — sharp corners for geometric areas and smooth curves for organic, flowing sections. It may take a few tries to get a feel for how the curve handles work, so do not get discouraged if it feels awkward at first.

docs.title: Step 7: Continue Adding Points to Build Your Shape
docs.text:
Once you have placed your first few anchor points, you can continue building your shape by adding more. The more anchor points you place, the more detailed and complex your shape can become — think of it like connecting the dots, where each click adds a new dot and the path follows behind connecting them all.

  1. Continue clicking around the canvas to add more anchor points in the direction you want your shape to go
  2. Click and drag at each new point to add smooth curves wherever your shape needs to turn or flow naturally

  3. Keep adding points until your shape outline is almost complete — you should be able to see the rough shape forming from the connected paths

    Figure 10: Building a custom path with multiple anchor points

Note

Do not worry if your first attempt does not look perfect — the Pen Tool genuinely takes practice and even experienced designers find it tricky at first. You can always go back and adjust individual anchor points after you close the path, which is covered in Step 9.

docs.title: Step 8: Close the Path
docs.text:
An open path is just a line — it has a starting point and an ending point that are not connected to each other. To turn your path into a proper closed shape that can be filled with a solid color, you need to connect your last anchor point back to the very first one you placed. This action is called closing the path and is required before you can apply any fill color.

  1. Hover your cursor slowly over the very first anchor point you placed — watch carefully for a small circle icon to appear next to the pen cursor, which signals that you are positioned correctly to close the path

  2. Click on the first anchor point to close the path and complete your shape

    Figure 11: Hovering over the first anchor point to close the path

    Figure 12: A completed closed vector path

  3. Confirm the path is now fully closed — it will form a complete enclosed shape with no open gaps

Warning

If you do not close the path, Photoshop will treat it as an open line rather than a filled shape. Always look for the small circle icon next to your cursor before clicking — if you do not see it, you are not hovering precisely enough over the first anchor point.

docs.title: Step 9: Adjust Your Vector Shape Using the Direct Selection Tool
docs.text:
One of the biggest advantages of working with vectors is that you can always go back and fine-tune your shape after drawing it. The Direct Selection Tool lets you click on any individual anchor point and drag it to a new position, reshaping that part of your vector without affecting the rest of the shape or having to redraw anything from scratch.

Figure 13: A closed vector path ready for editing

  1. Select the Direct Selection Tool by pressing Shift + A on your keyboard — your cursor will change to a white arrow to indicate the tool is active

Figure 14: Direct selection tool

  1. Click any anchor point on your shape to select it — the selected anchor point will turn solid blue, while unselected points remain hollow
  2. Drag the selected anchor point to a new position to adjust that portion of your shape — the surrounding paths will automatically update to follow the moved point

Figure 15: Preview of step 2 and 3

Note

The small hollow dots on your shape are anchor points, and the lines connecting them are paths. Moving a single anchor point only affects the paths directly connected to it, so you can make very precise adjustments to just one part of your shape without disturbing the rest of the outline.

Note

If you cannot see the anchor points, click directly on the shape first with the Direct Selection Tool to make them visible.

docs.title: Step 10: Save Your File
docs.text:
To preserve all of your vector layers and shapes, you must save your file in Photoshop's native format. Saving in any other image format will permanently flatten your layers into regular pixels, meaning you will no longer be able to select, resize, or edit your vector shapes in the future.

  1. Click [File] → [Save As]
  2. Set the format to .psd — this is Photoshop's native file format that preserves all layers, vector data, and edit history
  3. Click [Save] to save your file

Warning

Saving as .jpg or .png will permanently flatten your vector layers into pixels and you will lose the ability to scale or edit your shapes without quality loss. Always save your working file as .psd, and only export to .jpg or .png when you have a finished version that you no longer need to edit.

docs.title: Bonus Challenge
docs.text:
Now that you know the basics of both the Shape Tool and the Pen Tool, put your skills to the test! The images below show two complex vector shapes alongside their underlying anchor point structures on the right side — study the position of each anchor point carefully before attempting to recreate the shapes using the Pen Tool.

Figure 16: Challenge — recreate the star and clover shapes using the Pen Tool

Figure 17: Challenge — recreate the Christmas tree shape using the Pen Tool

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: You have successfully completed the Vector Shapes tutorial. You now know how to create precise vector shapes using the Shape Tool, draw fully custom vector paths using the Pen Tool, and fine-tune your shapes using the Direct Selection Tool. Because vectors are defined by mathematical paths rather than pixels, your shapes will always remain sharp and editable at any size — making these skills an essential foundation for any graphic design or illustration work you do in Photoshop.
